Speaking: Retell a story in sequence using beginning, middle, and end
Describe a person, place, or thing using sensory details
Ask and answer questions about a topic using complete sentences
Share an opinion and give one reason to support it
Give a two-step oral instruction in the correct order
Listening: Identify the main topic of a read-aloud using key details
Retell the key details of an informational text after listening
Follow two-step spoken directions in the correct order
Ask a clarifying question after listening to a short presentation
Identify how a speaker uses tone to show feeling
Reading: Identify the main idea of a short text and one supporting detail
Describe the connection between two events in a story
Use context clues to determine the meaning of an unfamiliar word
Identify the difference between fiction and informational text
Ask and answer questions about a text using evidence from the page
Writing: Write a sentence that states an opinion and gives one reason
Write a sequence of two events using transition words
Write a descriptive sentence using at least two sensory details
Write a question and a matching answer about a topic
Write a simple informational sentence about a familiar subject

Speaking: Present a main idea with two supporting details in logical order
Use transition words to connect ideas when speaking
Explain a process step by step in your own words
Respond to a peer's idea by agreeing or disagreeing with a reason
Adjust volume and pace to match the purpose of a presentation
Listening: Summarise a speaker's main idea in one or two sentences
Identify two supporting details from an oral presentation
Evaluate whether a speaker's reason supports their claim
Take brief notes while listening to an informational talk
Identify how visual aids support a speaker's message
Reading: Identify the main idea and two supporting details of an informational text
Compare and contrast two characters using text evidence
Determine the meaning of a figurative expression in context
Explain how an author uses text structure to organise information
Summarise a passage without including personal opinion
Writing: Write a paragraph with a clear topic sentence and two supporting details
Use transition words to connect ideas across sentences
Write a conclusion sentence that restates the main idea
Revise a sentence to improve word choice and clarity
Write a compare and contrast paragraph using a graphic organiser

Speaking: Present a claim and support it with two pieces of evidence
Use precise academic vocabulary appropriate to the topic
Acknowledge a counterclaim and explain why your position is stronger
Organise a spoken argument using a clear claim, evidence, and conclusion
Use eye contact and deliberate pacing to engage an audience
Listening: Evaluate the strength of evidence used to support a speaker's claim
Identify the difference between a speaker's opinion and factual evidence
Summarise a multi-point argument in two or three sentences
Identify logical fallacies or weak reasoning in a spoken argument
Analyse how a speaker's word choice shapes the tone of a message
Reading: Analyse how a central idea develops across paragraphs in a text
Evaluate the strength of an author's argument using text evidence
Determine the connotative meaning of words in an argumentative text
Compare how two authors present different perspectives on the same topic
Identify the author's purpose and explain how structure supports it
Writing: Write a claim statement that is specific and arguable
Integrate a direct quote as evidence and explain its relevance
Write a rebuttal paragraph that addresses a counterclaim
Use formal academic language appropriate for an argumentative essay
Write a conclusion that synthesises the argument rather than restating it

Speaking: Construct and deliver a persuasive argument using logos, ethos, and pathos
Anticipate and address audience objections within a spoken argument
Use rhetorical devices such as repetition or antithesis to strengthen a point
Synthesise evidence from multiple sources into a coherent spoken argument
Adapt register and tone for a specific audience and purpose
Listening: Evaluate the logical structure of a complex spoken argument
Identify rhetorical strategies a speaker uses to persuade an audience
Assess the credibility and relevance of evidence in a spoken presentation
Synthesise ideas from two speakers presenting opposing viewpoints
Identify how a speaker's tone and delivery influence audience perception
Reading: Analyse how an author builds a sustained argument across an entire text
Evaluate the validity and sufficiency of evidence in a persuasive text
Identify and analyse the effect of rhetorical devices in a written text
Compare the argumentation strategies of two texts on the same topic
Determine how an author's background and purpose shape their perspective
Writing: Write a thesis statement that makes a complex, defensible claim
Develop an argument using evidence from at least two distinct sources
Write a paragraph that addresses and refutes a nuanced counterclaim
Use varied syntax and precise diction to strengthen persuasive writing
Write a conclusion that extends the argument beyond the scope of the essay
